claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To begin, I need to ask you about the child or children you're claiming for.

**How old is your child?** 

The age is important because:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically (subject to other conditions)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** typically don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age, and if you're claiming for multiple children, let me know about all of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex, he's 8 years old and lives with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Since Alex is 8 years old (under 16), let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, the next question:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Since Alex is only 8 years old, I expect he's not working at all, which would mean the answer is "yes" - but please confirm this for me.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, he's only 8 - he doesn't work at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

In other words, does Alex receive any benefits like ESA or Universal Credit directly in his own name? For an 8-year-old, this would be very unusual, so I expect the answer is that he does NOT receive these benefits (which would be "yes" to this question).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Next:

**Is Alex NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?**

Since Alex is only 8 years old, he wouldn't be in an apprenticeship at all. So the answer here would be that he's "not in an apprenticeship."

Can you confirm Alex is not in any type of apprenticeship program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct, he's not in any apprenticeship - he's just 8, so he's in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to ask about your residency status:

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if **ANY** of the following applies to you:

1. You normally live in the UK
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (for up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself and your family financially (and NOT claiming certain benefits)
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these situations apply to you?** Most commonly, people qualify simply because they normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Next question:

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You mentioned earlier that Alex lives with you at home, which means you meet the responsibility requirement. 

You're considered responsible for a child if **ANY** of the following applies:
- You live with the child ✓ (This applies to you!)
- You contribute to their upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care)
- You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Since Alex lives with you, can you confirm that you are responsible for him?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely - he lives with me and I look after him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to ask:

**Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. This is important to check because:
- If no one else is claiming, we can continue
- If someone else is claiming but you both agree that you should claim instead, that can be arranged
- If there's a dispute about who should claim, HMRC would need to decide

Is anyone else (such as Alex's other parent or another family member) currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to understand Alex's living situation:

**Where is Alex currently living?**

I need to check if any special circumstances apply:
- Is Alex in hospital?
- Is Alex in local authority care?
- Is Alex being fostered?
- Or is Alex living normally at home with you?

You mentioned earlier that Alex lives with you at home in Manchester. Can you confirm that he's living normally at home with you (not in hospital, care, or a fostering arrangement)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually, I am fostering Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for sharing that important information. Since you're fostering Alex, I need to ask some follow-up questions because there are specific rules for foster carers.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is an important question for fostering situations:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words:
- If the local council **is paying** you for Alex's accommodation or maintenance (as is typical for foster care arrangements), the answer would be "no"
- If the local council is **NOT paying** for his accommodation or maintenance, the answer would be "yes"

Most foster carers receive fostering allowances from the local council, which would mean the council IS paying towards the child's accommodation and maintenance.

**Is the local council paying you a fostering allowance or other payments for Alex's care?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I don't get anything from the council for fostering him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! One final confirmation:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

You've just confirmed that you don't receive any payments from the council for fostering Alex. This means the answer to this question is "yes" - the council is NOT paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

Can you confirm this is correct - that the local council is not providing any financial support for Alex's care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - the council isn't paying anything towards his care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News - You're Eligible!

**You meet all the criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.**

### Why You're Eligible:

✅ **Alex is a qualifying child** - At 8 years old, he's under 16  
✅ **You meet UK residency requirements** - You normally live in the UK (Manchester)  
✅ **You're responsible for Alex** - He lives with you and you look after him  
✅ **No one else is claiming** - No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex  
✅ **Council not paying** - Crucially, even though you're fostering Alex, the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance, which means you can claim Child Benefit

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay some or all of it back through tax
5. **Payment frequency** - Payments are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### How to Apply:
Visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

If you have any other questions about the application process, please let me know!

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 8 years old (under 16), which qualifies as a qualifying child; (2) He is not working (children under 16 working 24+ hours per week would be excluded); (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right such as ESA or Universal Credit; (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) The claimant meets UK residency requirements by normally living in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as they live together; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is being fostered but the local council is NOT paying towards his accommodation or maintenance, which means the claimant can claim Child Benefit for him; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's care in any other capacity. All eligibility requirements have been met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is fostering one child, Alex (age 8), in Manchester. The key factor in this case is that while Alex is in a fostering arrangement, the local council is not providing any financial support for his accommodation or maintenance. Under UK Child Benefit rules, foster carers can claim Child Benefit for fostered children if the council is not paying towards their care. Since all other eligibility criteria are met (qualifying child, residency, responsibility, no other claimant, and no council payments), the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 8 years old (under 16), which qualifies as a qualifying child; (2) He is not working (children under 16 working 24+ hours per week would be excluded); (3) He is not receiving benefits in his own right such as ESA or Universal Credit; (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) The claimant meets UK residency requirements by normally living in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as they live together; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is being fostered but the local council is NOT paying towards his accommodation or maintenance, which means the claimant can claim Child Benefit for him; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's care in any other capacity. All eligibility requirements have been met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is fostering one child, Alex (age 8), in Manchester. The key factor in this case is that while Alex is in a fostering arrangement, the local council is not providing any financial support for his accommodation or maintenance. Under UK Child Benefit rules, foster carers can claim Child Benefit for fostered children if the council is not paying towards their care. Since all other eligibility criteria are met (qualifying child, residency, responsibility, no other claimant, and no council payments), the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
